flashcards.front: What is the Von Neumann architecture?
flashcards.back: A computer design model where both program instructions and data are stored in memory. The CPU fetches instructions from memory one at a time, decodes them, and executes them sequentially.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the System Bus in computer organization?
flashcards.back: The system bus connects the CPU, memory, and I/O devices. It carries address, data, and control signals between components, enabling communication and data transfer.

flashcards.front: What is an interrupt in computer systems?
flashcards.back: An interrupt is a signal sent to the CPU by hardware or software indicating an event that needs immediate attention. The CPU suspends its current task and executes an interrupt service routine.
DMA allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention. The DMA controller manages the transfer, freeing the CPU to perform other tasks.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of Cache Memory?
flashcards.back: Cache memory is a small, fast memory located between the CPU and main memory. It stores frequently accessed data and instructions to reduce memory access time and improve system performance.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Harvard and Von Neumann architectures?
flashcards.back: Harvard architecture uses separate memory spaces for instructions and data, allowing simultaneous access to both. Von Neumann uses a single memory for both, causing the von Neumann bottleneck.

flashcards.front: What is a Bus Cycle in computer organization?
flashcards.back: A bus cycle is the basic unit of time for bus operations. It includes the time to send an address, read/write data, and receive acknowledgment. Multiple clock cycles may be needed per bus cycle.

flashcards.front: What is Polling as an I/O method?
flashcards.back: Polling is an I/O technique where the CPU repeatedly checks each I/O device for readiness. The CPU stays in a loop querying devices, which is inefficient compared to interrupts.
A device controller is hardware that manages communication between the CPU and I/O devices. It interprets commands, performs data buffering, and handles device-specific operations.
The bootstrap loader is a small program stored in ROM that executes when the computer powers on. It initializes hardware and loads the operating system into memory.
A multi-processor system has multiple CPUs (processors) that share memory and I/O devices. It provides higher processing power, reliability, and scalability through parallel processing.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between SIMD and MIMD architectures?
flashcards.back: SIMD (Single Instruction Multiple Data) executes the same instruction on multiple data items simultaneously. MIMD (Multiple Instruction Multiple Data) allows each processor to execute different instructions on different data.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of Memory-Mapped I/O?
flashcards.back: Memory-mapped I/O assigns addresses to I/O devices so they can be accessed like regular memory locations. The CPU uses the same instructions to access both memory and I/O devices.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the System Clock?
flashcards.back: The system clock generates regular timing pulses that synchronize all computer operations. It coordinates CPU cycles, bus transfers, and ensures components work in proper sequence.
The motherboard is the main circuit board that houses the CPU, memory, I/O controllers, and expansion slots. It provides the physical and electrical connections between all computer components.
